48619279.simonmed_report_text:
INDICATION:  Pulmonary sarcoidosis, mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

COMPARISON:  Chest CT on December 19, 2024.

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 10.91 mGy; total DLP 387.18 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  Majority of the previously seen pulmonary nodules are not identified. A few residual micronodules are stable for example left lower lobe image 162. No new consolidation, emphysematous changes or bronchiectasis. Multiple thoracolumbar vertebral thickening.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  Interval decrease in size and number of mediastinal lymph nodes currently subcentimeter. Right PICC catheter tip terminates in cavoatrial junction.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Mild degenerative changes and mild scoliosis of the thoracic spine.

Upper abdomen:  Cholecystectomy.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Near-complete resolution of previously seen pulmonary nodules. A few residual scattered micronodules. Follow-up with CT chest in 12 months to ensure stability in a high-risk patient, otherwise no follow-up imaging is required.
2. Decrease in size and number of mediastinal lymph nodes.
3. Moderate coronary artery atherosclerotic calcifications..


DISCLAIMER:  AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

49613094.simonmed_report_text:
INDICATION:  Cough for 6 weeks. Acute upper respiratory infection.

COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph March 3, 2026.

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 5.34 mGy; total DLP 226.96 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura: Biapical pleuroparenchymal scarring. Medial right middle lobe subpleural irregular area of consolidation/atelectasis with air bronchograms measuring 2.7 × 2.0 × 2.5 cm. In the consolidation area there are a single calcification up to 2 mm and bronchiectasis
. On the periphery of the consolidation area there are bronchocele, tree-in-bud nodules, ground glass areas.
Lingular/left upper lobe, subpleural area of consolidation with similar characteristics, measuring up to 1.2 cm, with surrounding ground-glass opacity.
On both sides, polysegmental confluent centrilobular nodules (tree-in-bud) with areas of ground glass at the periphery. Inspissated secretions in subsegmental bronchi.  No pleural effusion.


AI-Detected Nodules (Verified by Radiologist): 

Right lower lobe Pure Ground Glass Nodule: 5.3 mm = 5.9 mm x 4.8 mm (5/211).

Left lower lobe Pure Ground Glass Nodule: 4.6 mm = 5.6 mm x 3.6 mm (5/193). 

Left lower lobe Mixed Ground Glass Nodule: 4.2 mm = 5.2 mm x 3.2 mm (5/222).

Left upper lobe Pure Ground Glass Nodule: 4.7 mm = 5.0 mm x 4.3 mm (5/173). 

Right lower lobe Pure Ground Glass Nodule: 4.0 mm = 4.7 mm x 3.3 mm (5/216).


Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels:  Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Sclerotic lesions in the bodies of the T5 and T9 vertebrae 1.1 cm and 1.0 cm respectively. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

An ovoid lesion in the left breast 1.1 × 1.0 cm.

Upper abdomen:  Unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 
1. Multifocal of bilateral areas of consolidation, bronchiectasis and bronchocentric tree-in-bud micronodules are consistent with infectious/inflammatory process, most consistent with non-tuberculous mycobacterial (MAC) infection likely acute on chronic. (Lady Windermere syndrome). Recommend follow-up according to Fleischner guidelines to ensure complete resolution.> 8mm: CT at 3-6 months, then at 18-24 months
2. Left breast lesion. Correlate with the dedicated breast imaging.



DISCLAIMER:  AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

COMPARISON:  None available.

TECHNIQUE:  Thin section axial CT through the thorax without intravenous contrast utilizing a low dose protocol. Coronal, sagittal and MIP reformations were generated on the same workstation. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 4.94  mGy; total DLP 122.63 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  Mild paraseptal emphysema in the apices.  Calcified granulomas measuring up to 3 mm of both lungs. Respiratory motion artifacts are present in the lung bases. Linear atelectasis in the left upper lobe. Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe and in the lingular and basal segments of the left lung. No pleural effusion.

Trachea: The membranous portion of the trachea prolapses into the lumen (study likely performed on incomplete inspiration). Small amount of secretion within the trachea.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Subjectively, there is mild coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated. There is mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Upper abdomen:  There is hepatic steatosis.

IMPRESSION:

1. No nodules requiring follow-up according to Fleischner Society recommendations.

2. Mild, upper lobe predominant paraseptal emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. 

3. Calcified healed granulomas of both lungs.

4. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Mild aortic calcification.  

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with diffuse calcification of the anterior longitudinal ligament.

47603528.simonmed_report_text:
INDICATION:  Solitary pulmonary nodule.

COMPARISON:  None available.

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 7.37 mGy; total DLP 255.22 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema, with an upper lobe predominance.  Interlobular septal thickening in the lung bases with subpleural reticulation. There is mild, heterogeneous groundglass density is well.

Notable pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows: 

Right upper lobe:
*        2 mm solid nodule in image 93.
*        7 mm nodule along the minor fissure in image 129.

Left upper lobe:
*        3 mm solid nodule in image 76.
*        2 mm solid nodule in image 88.
*        3 mm solid nodule in image 113.
*        5 mm solid nodule in image 155.

Left lower lobe:
*        5 mm solid nodule in image 159.

 Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. Small pericardial effusion. Fatty inclusions of the myocardium of the right heart. Subjectively, there is mild left circumflex and left anterior descending coronary calcification. There is mild to moderate right coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Mild aortic calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Healed fractures of the left 10th and 11th ribs. In the 4th rib on the right there is mild, benign appearing sclerosis.

Upper abdomen:  Mild thickening of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland without discrete nodularity. Otherwise unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Multiple solid pulmonary nodules in both lungs measuring up to 7 mm.  ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: For patients with low risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, no further follow-up or an optional chest CT at 18-24 months if there is high index of suspicion. For patients with high risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, another chest CT at 18-24 months.

2. Bilateral regions of basilar predominant ground-glass opacity and interstitial thickening could be cardiogenic. Consider cardiac echo.

3. Moderate, upper lobe predominant centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

4. Mild pericardial effusion, mild bilateral pleural effusions might be cardiogenic or a manifestation of an inflammatory disease.

5. Fatty inclusions of the myocardium of the right heart. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.


DISCLAIMER:  AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

Management depends on clinical evaluation, patient preference, and the probability of malignancy.

3. Small pericardial effusion. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic and mitral valve calcifications.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. There is mild coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcification of the aortic arch and descending thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.2 cm.

Marginal anterior and lateral osteophytes along the vertebral body margins are present. Disruption of the endplates of isolated vertebral bodies consistent with Schmorl nodes. - For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Mild coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Aortic calcification.

4. Main pulmonary artery dilation may indicate pulmonary arterial hypertension.

5. Solitary hepatic calcification may be secondary to a prior granulomatous process. Pancreatic focal lipomatosis. Cholecystectomy.

6. Hemangioma in the T11 vertebral body. Degenerative changes of thoracic spine. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteoporosis. Healing fractures of the left 10th and 11th ribs.

7. Thyroid nodule up to 7 mm, per “Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee,” 2014, no additional evaluation is required.

49564884.simonmed_report_text:
INDICATION:  Abnormal findings of lung field. Removed part of lung because of cancer.

COMPARISON:  Chest x-ray 3/10/2026.

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 14.17 mGy; total DLP 534.10 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  
-Status post resection of the upper right lobe with metal sutures are visualized along the mediastinum and the right upper lobar bronchus. On both sides of the metal sutures, a solid central mass is visualized, measuring approximately 5.6 x 3.2 x 6.0 cm, coursing along the middle and lower lobes, the lumen of the bronchial stump, with probable invasion of the mediastinum. Stump of the right upper lobar bronchus with thickening of the wall. Along the suture material in the right middle lobe there is an area of consolidation with smooth edges, 5.0 x 2.3 cm.
-Moderate paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema. In the lower lobes of both lungs there are multiple centrilobular areas of ground glass, mosaic pneumatization with air traps, the contents in the lumens of the subsegmental bronchi (small airways disease). In the lower lobe of the right lung there are linear opacities, may be an inflammatory process or hypoventilatory atelectasis. In the left lung subpleural reticulation and ground glass, consistent with interstitial changes.
-In the left lung, there are a few nodules, on series 5 as follows:  
In the left upper lobe:  
* Solid nodule up to 4 mm (image 134).  
* Solid nodule up to 3 mm (image 100).  
In the left lower lobe:  
* Subsolid nodule up to 7 mm (image 146).  
On the right, there is a small amount of fluid in the pleural space (thickness about 1.2 cm); on the left, there is no effusion. Right pleura is thickened up to 8 mm.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  Mediastinum is mildly shifted to the right, not unexpected status post right upper lobectomy. Mediastinal lymph nodes are numerous, the largest short axis up to 1.4 cm ((series 2 image 36). Right bronchopulmonary lymph nodes are difficult to differentiate on the background of probable invasion of the mass.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly or significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.

Great vessels:  Thoracic aorta is nondilated. There is mild atherosclerotic plaque. Main pulmonary artery is at the upper limits of normal measuring 3.1 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Multiple small sclerotic lesions of the right 5th, 6th, 7th, 8th ribs. Fractures of the posterior segment of the right sixth rib and middle segment of the seventh rib with displacement of bone fragments. Decreased vertebral body height at T6–T9 up to 32%, decreased vertebral density at T11–L1 (58–61 HU). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Mild gynecomastia.

Upper abdomen:  In the mid third of the right kidney parenchyma in the posterior segment, a hypodense lesion with sharp smooth margins and fluid attenuation, measuring about 2.0 × 1.5 cm. No follow-up imaging recommended as incidental renal lesions without suspicious features are likely benign.

Included neck: Right supraclavicular lymph nodes up to 1.0 cm in short axis.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Recurrence of a lung neoplasm, located centrally in the area of ??the stump of the right upper lobar bronchus, with mediastinal lymphadenopathy and probable mediastinal invasion. Recurrent disease extends along the suture margin and involves the right middle and lower lobes.

2. Few solid and one subsolid nodules in the left lung. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history. Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.

3. Moderate mixed emphysema. In the lower lobes of both lungs there are signs of small airways disease. In the lower lobe of the right lung there are linear opacities, may be an inflammatory process or hypoventilatory atelectasis. Mild effusion in the right pleural space.

4. Multiple small osteoblastic lesions of the right 5th, 6th, 7th, 8th ribs. Fractures of the posterior segment of the right sixth rib and middle segment of the seventh rib with displacement of bone fragments, may be pathological fractures.

5. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.  Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

Given the positive findings, this examination was assigned to the SimonMed assistant to be communicated to the ordering physician or their clinician's representative, in addition to immediate report transmission via the electronic medical record interface or fax at the time of dictation.



DISCLAIMER:  AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

In the right lower lobe, there is progression of ground-glass opacity (series 4, image 193) measuring 2.6 × 2.4 cm (previously 1.5 × 1.5 cm).  

Also, there are persistent multiple pulmonary nodules (series 4) and perifissural nodes:  
In the right upper lobe:

In the left lower lobe:

In the left upper lobe (series 5):

* 5 mm solid nodule (image 61).
In the right lower lobe (series 5):
* 3 mm solid nodule (image 113).

In the right lung, single micronodules are present. Linear atelectasis in the lower portions of both lungs in the setting of a hiatal hernia. No pleural effusion.

In the right liver lobe a single hypodense lesion up to 7 × 6 mm with indistinct margins (series 3, image 185).

Multiple ground-glass and solid nodules in both lungs measure up to 5 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

Minimal calcification of the aortic valve and moderate calcification of the mitral valve. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic and abdominal aorta.

3. Small hypodense lesion in the right liver lobe with indistinct margins. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, follow-up MRI in 3 to 6 months is recommended for further evaluation.

4. Hiatal hernia without evidence of strangulation, causing minimal compressive changes of the adjacent lung parenchyma.

5. Calcified colonic diverticula without acute diverticulitis.

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 4.

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  In the left lung, there are multiple centrilobular nodules, peribronchial zones of consolidation and ground glass opacity with thickening of the interlobular interstitium, with mild worsening compared to 2/11/2026. There is a greater degree of distribution and confluence on the current study.  On the right in all lobes, small centrilobular confluent nodules of ground glass; previously, the centrilobular nodules were denser, without the formation of large areas of ground glass. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass and no lymphadenopathy. There is uneven dilatation of the esophageal lumen with a large amount of fluid content. There is a hiatal hernia with herniation of a portion of the stomach into the inferior posterior mediastinum, measuring 4.9 × 3.8 cm.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is dilated to 4.2 cm at the level of the ascending portion, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.5 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Moderate degenerative changes and scoliosis of the thoracic spine.

Upper abdomen:  There is a small cyst arising from the superior pole the left kidney only partially included in the field-of-view. No follow-up imaging recommended as incidental renal lesions without suspicious features are likely benign. Moderate-sized hiatal hernia.

Thyroid gland: in the right lobe of the thyroid gland there is a heterogeneous lesion with calcification, with substernal extension, measuring up to 2.1 × 2.5 cm. Hypodense lesion of the left lobe of the thyroid gland, 7 mm (no need for further follow-up).

IMPRESSION: 

1. Interval mild to moderate worsening of left lung centrilobular nodules, peribronchial consolidation, and ground-glass opacities with interlobular septal thickening, suggestive of worsening chronic infection (with etiologies including but not limited to: aspiration pneumonia, atypical infections or chronic bronchiolitis).

2. Moderate hiatal hernia. There is a patulous appearance of the esophagus containing an air/fluid level, which could reflect sequelae of reflux. This can predispose the patient to aspiration. Consider modified barium swallow study/esophagram.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. 

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. Dilatation of ascending aorta up to 4.2 cm. Recommend cardiology follow-up and imaging surveillance. If no prior imaging is available, consider repeat CTA or MRA in ~12 months to establish stability.

5. Dilatation of the main pulmonary artery, which may suggest pulmonary hypertension. Consider cardiac echo.

6. Heterogeneous lesion of the right thyroid lobe, with calcification and substernal extension. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained.

49341756.simonmed_report_text:
INDICATION:  Thoracic aortic aneurysm.

COMPARISON:  None available.

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 14.91 mGy; total DLP 55.98 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  In the right lung perifissural nodules are present along the oblique and horizontal interlobar fissures, up to 4 mm in diameter. In the left upper lobe, solid nodule measures up to 3 mm. In both lungs pulmonary cysts are present, up to 9 mm in diameter. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels:  Ascending thoracic aorta is dilated (42 mm). Main pulmonary artery is dilated (30 mm). Scattered calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Decreased height and wedge deformity of the vertebral bodies at T11 up to 32% and at T12 up to 28%. Moderate degenerative changes of thoracic spine and shoulder joints.

Upper abdomen:  Unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Ascending thoracic aortic dilatation, up to 42 mm. Moderate calcification of the aorta.

2. Perifissural nodules in the right lung do not require follow-up. In the left upper lobe, solid nodule measures up to 3 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion.

3. Pulmonary cysts in both lungs, if asymptomatic, it may not have clinical significance.

4. Main pulmonary artery dilatation to 3.0 cm, may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.

5. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

6. Wedge deformity of the T11 and T12 vertebral bodies may be a sign of osteoporosis. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and of the shoulder joints.


DISCLAIMER:  AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

In the right middle lobe:  
* Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 146).  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 166).  
In the left upper lobe:  
* Nodule along the interlobar pleura measuring up to 4 mm (image 55).

49658402.simonmed_report_text:
INDICATION:  Pulmonary nodules.

COMPARISON:  Chest CT on September 23, 2025, 3/26/2025 and 8/22/2024.

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 13.44 mGy; total DLP 465.3 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 2

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:
Solid nodule measures 4 mm in the right upper lobe (series 5, image 23), unchanged.  
Solid nodule measures 9 mm in the right upper lobe (series 5, image 78), unchanged.
Solid nodule measures 9 mm in the right middle lobe (series 5, image 131), unchanged.
Solid nodule measures 6 mm in the left lower lobe (series 5, image 140), unchanged.
Solid nodule measures 4 mm in the left lower lobe (series 5, image 97), unchanged.
5 mm solid nodule in image 56, left upper lobe, no significant change.
In both lungs, few solid nodules up to 2 mm, stable.
In both lungs, multiple cysts; the largest in the middle lobe (1.0 × 0.9 cm), stable. Subpleural reticulations in the middle lobe and lingular segments, in lung bases, unchanged. Linear atelectasis in the middle lobe and lingular segments on the left (unchanged, clinically insignificant). No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass and no lymphadenopathy.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels:  Thoracic aorta is not dilated; moderate wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is at the upper limits of normal measuring 3.0 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Anterolisthesis of T2 on T3 by 5 mm. 

Upper abdomen:  Fat-containing lesion (7 mm) of the left kidney, not requiring further work-up, likely an angiomyolipoma or focal cortical scarring. Renal sinus cyst of the left kidney (1.5 cm). No follow-up imaging recommended as incidental renal lesions without suspicious features are likely benign.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Multiple bilateral nodules are without significant change compared to 3/26/2025, measuring up to 9 mm. Due to the presence of cystic lung disease it may represent multifocal micronodular pneumocyte hyperplasia that is associated with tuberous sclerosis / lymphangiomyomatosis.  ACR Lung-RADS guidelines suggest the following: Category 2 (Benign appearance or behavior). Continue annual screening with Low Dose chest CT in 12 months.  

2.  Diffuse cystic lung disease is without significant change. Consider lymphangioleiomyomatosis/tuberous sclerosis (Managing Incidental Findings on Thoracic CT: Lung Findings. A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2024).

3. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Thoracic aortic calcification.

4. Main pulmonary artery is at the upper limits of normal. Consider cardiac echo to evaluate for pulmonary artery hypertension.



DISCLAIMER:  AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  Solid subpleural nodule in the right lower lobe, measuring up to 1×1 cm (series 5, image 121), stable. Ground glass micronodules in the upper lobe of the left lung, stable. Moderate centrilobular emphysema in both lungs, predominantly in the upper lungs. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated (3.4 cm).

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. There is a nonaggressive appearing 6 mm osteosclerotic lesion in T3 vertebral arch.

45942123.simonmed_report_text:
INDICATION:  Coccidioidomycosis, unspecified.

COMPARISON:  Chest CT on August 28, 2023.

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 9.16 mGy; total DLP 325.19 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  Clustered centrilobular nodules persist in the left lower lobe, forming a “tree-in-bud” pattern, compared with the prior study with decreased size:
- The largest nodule previously up to 1.3 × 0.8 cm (series 5, image 112) has decreased to 6 mm on the current study, is less rounded, and has more linear margins.
- The largest nodule on the current study is up to 8 mm (series 5, image 104), previously up to 9 mm.
The remaining previously noted solid and calcified nodules (single) show no interval change, shown on series 5:
In the right lung:
- In the upper lobe, a subpleural nodule up to 3 mm, partially calcified (image 68).
- In the middle lobe, a subpleural nodule up to 3 mm (image 103).
Single calcifications in the left upper lobe up to 5 mm (no interval change).
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  Partly calcified aortopulmonary window lymph node measures 1.5 cm in short axis,  no interval change. Nonenlarged lower paratracheal lymph node with single calcifications, no interval change.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcifications. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. Decrease in vertebral body density: T11 - 148 HU, T12 - 129 HU, L1 - 101 HU, L2 - 100 HU.

Upper abdomen:  Calcification in the right hepatic lobe up to 5 mm (no interval change). No need for further follow-up.

Thyroid: Heterogeneous structure due to nodules; the largest on the left is up to 2.9 × 2.7 cm (no interval change), with mild extension into the superior mediastinum. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Decreased in size, clustered centrilobular nodules in the left lower lobe, consistent with sequelae of known coccidiomycosis. No new pulmonary nodules. Stable solid and partially calcified pulmonary nodules. Per Fleischner Society recommendations 2017, no further follow-up is required.

2. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. No coronary artery calcification.

3. Stable partly calcified mediastinal lymph nodes, sequelae of granulomatous disease.

4. Stable thyroid nodules. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia.


DISCLAIMER:  AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

49733883.simonmed_report_text:
INDICATION:  Check up. Reported history of triple bypass surgery 08/24/2025.

COMPARISON:  Chest x-ray 10/17/2025

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 18.23 mGy; total DLP 648 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  In the right lung, solitary micronodules up to 2 mm. Mild centrilobular emphysema with few areas of mosaic attenuation. In the right lower lobe there is a paravertebral area of osteophyte-induced pulmonary fibrosis, in the right middle lobe, lingular and basal segments there are linear atelectasis (clinically not significant). Biapical scarring. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass or adenopathy is identified. Soft tissue infiltration surrounding the median sternotomy, likely reflecting healing. No drainable fluid collection. Small hiatal hernia. 

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Thickening of the pericardium up to 3.5 mm. Severe native three-vessel coronary artery calcification. Sequelae of coronary artery bypass graft. Calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is mildly dilated to 3.1 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Sequelae of median sternotomy, healing. Mild, symmetric gynecomastia.

Upper abdomen:  Multiple diverticula of the transverse colon and descending colon. Hypodense (-1 HU) left adrenal lesion with smooth well-defined margins, measuring up to 1.1 cm. Cholecystectomy.

IMPRESSION: 

1. There is sequelae of healing median sternotomy, with mild infiltration of the surrounding soft tissues. No drainable fluid collection.

2. Tiny pulmonary nodules are present. Per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, no additional CT follow-up is recommended.

3. Mild centrilobular emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

4. Calcification of the aortic and mitral valves. Calcification of the thoracic aorta. Dense native coronary artery calcification is not unexpected given the recent coronary artery bypass graft. 5. Mildly dilated main pulmonary artery to 3.1 cm. This could reflect pulmonary artery hypertension. Correlation with cardiac echo is recommended.

5. Left adrenal lesion measures up to 1.1 cm, -1 HU. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: Benign adenoma. No need for further imaging follow-up. 



DISCLAIMER:  AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

J Am Coll Radiol 2018;15:264-273) suggest the following: Further evaluation with CT or MR without and with IV contrast recommended. Hemorrhagic or proteinaceous cyst of the left kidney. Per ACR White Paper recommendations, no further evaluation is required. Nonobstructing right renal calculus.

J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: For patients without cancer history, follow-up study with adrenal protocol CT in 12 months recommended. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status and exclude pheochromocytoma. For patients with positive cancer history, prompt further evaluation with adrenal protocol CT recommended.
Left adrenal lesion measures 11 mm, 7 HU, benign lesion. No need for further imaging follow-up.

J AM Coll Radiol 2017; 14: 911-923) suggest the following: Follow-up pancreas-protocol CT or MRI with contrast at 2 years and 4 years after the initial discovery of the cyst or after a growing cyst becomes stable again.

Bilateral diffuse mosaic groundglass attenuation patterns representing air trapping versus perfusional abnormality, more pronounced in the lower lobes, middle lobe and lingual segments.

The walls of the bronchi of the lower lobes, middle lobe and lingual segments are severely thickened, subsegmental bronchi are obstructed by contents, in some bronchi by calcified contents.
Numerous foci of mucous stasis.
A cluster of bronchocentric tree-in-bud groundglass nodules in right upper lobe may represent active infection/inflammation (3/27).
In the upper lobes and to a lesser extent in the lower lobes, multiple centrilobular nodules and centrilobular ground-glass opacities.
Paraseptal subpleural emphysematous changes along the anterior aspect of right upper lobe.

 In the upper lobes, thickening of the interlobular interstitium with suggestion of pan lobar emphysema. Scattered noncalcified and calcified nodules in both lungs measure up to 6 mm in diameter for example 5/27. Mild central bronchiectasis and bronchiectasis predominantly involving the lower lobes.
Linear atelectasis against the background of bilateral Bochdalek hernias.
No consolidation or pleural effusion.
Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart:  The heart is enlarged, cardiothoracic ratio is 0.67. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively moderate. Calcification of the mitral and aortic valves.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques are present in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. 
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-T12 level to 122 HU (CT signs of demineralization). L1 retrolisthesis, 4 mm, grade I according to Meyerding classification.

Upper abdomen:  Diverticulosis of the transverse colon without signs of inflammation. In the upper pole of the left kidney, a calculus measures up to 2 mm in diameter.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Diffuse bronchial disease with mild central bronchiectasis, calcified and noncalcified nodules up to 6 mm (including tree-in-bud centrilobular nodules) and foci of mucostasis. Findings may represent chronic obliterating bronchiolitis or diffuse panbronchiolitis likely in the setting of atypical/granulomatous infection/inflammation with some active infection/inflammation in right upper lobe. Recommend follow-up according to Fleischner guidelines.
Low-Risk Patient, Multiple: 
< 6 mm: No routine follow-up 

High-Risk Patient, Multiple: 
<6 mm: Optional CT at 12 months

2. Suggestion of small airway disease/air trapping versus perfusional abnormality, incompletely assessed on this single phase exam. Recommend future  follow-ups with dual phase chest CT (Inspiratory/expiratory phase).

3. Cardiomegaly. Coronary calcification, qualitatively moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  Small areas of paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema are identified in the apices. A few calcified nodules are present in both lungs; the largest measures up to 4 mm in the left upper lobe, no follow-up is required.
Solid nodules are seen in the lung parenchyma, in series 4, as follows:
In the right lower lobe:
- Nodule measures up to 2 mm (image 115), unchanged.
- Nodule measures up to 3 mm (image 126), unchanged.
In the left lower lobe:
- Subpleural nodule measures up to 3 mm (image 148), previously not seen.
No pleural effusion. The costal pleura shows areas of localized thickening bilaterally up to 5 mm (unchanged), with calcification. An increase in the size of some calcifications is observed in time.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  Increased size of intrathoracic lymph nodes of the prevascular group with interval worsening. In series 3, an increase in the size of the lymph node on image 47 measures 1.5 cm (previously 4 mm), the lymph node on image 80 measures 1.2 cm (previously 4 mm); the lymph node on image 101 decreased to 1.1 cm (previously 1.5 cm).

Heart:  No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Mild aortic valve calcification. Presumed mitral valve replacement. Moderate coronary calcification.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Deformity of the inferior endplate of the L1 vertebral body due to a Schmorl node measuring up to 1.1 cm. Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-T12 level measures 114 HU (CT signs of demineralization). Severe degenerative changes of shoulder joints.

Upper abdomen:  Hypodense lesion in the liver parenchyma in segment 1 with well-defined smooth margins and fluid density measures up to 9 mm, unchanged. In the left kidney, two hypodense lesions with well-defined smooth margins and fluid density measure up to 1.8 × 1.5 cm and 2.0 × 1.9 cm. Lesion identified in the upper pole is previously not seen. Cholecystectomy. The common bile duct measures up to 12 mm, unchanged. In the lateral limb of the right adrenal gland, a fat-density lesion measures 8 mm, requires no follow follow-up, unchanged.

Thyroid gland: In the left lobe, a hypodense lesion measures up to 9 × 7 mm.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Compared to Chest CT on November 7, 2019, solid nodules in the right lower lobe are unchanged; a new solid nodule measures up to 3 mm in the left lower lobe. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion.

2. CT findings of mild centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria. The current study may be used as a baseline for subsequent follow-up.

3. Multiple calcified pleural plaques with interval increase in the size of some calcifications may represent asbestos-related pleural disease.

4. Intrathoracic lymphadenopathy (3A group) with interval worsening compared to Chest CT on November 7, 2019, may be asbestos-related or related to neoplastic disease.

5. Moderate coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

6. Mild aortic valve calcification.

7. Left renal cysts. Per “Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee” (2018), CT follow-up is not required.

8. Right adrenal lesion with a benign phenotype (likely myelolipoma), unchanged. Per “Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee” (2017), an incidental adrenal mass that is <1 cm needs no further work-up or follow-up.

9. Left thyroid nodule. Per “Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee” (2014), no need for further follow-up.

49223915.simonmed_report_text:
INDICATION:  History of lung cancer. History of non-Hodgkin's lymphoma of the abdomen.

COMPARISON:  Chest CT on June 26, 2024, December 11, 2024. CT chest 6/26/2024, 6/16/2022 and 5/8/2021.

TECHNIQUE:  Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 11.38 mGy; total DLP 471.82 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  In the apex of the right lung, a spiculated solid mass measures 3.4 × 2.3 × 1.7 cm, with strands to the pleura, with a single 1 mm calcification, deformation and interruption of the small bronchi. On December 11, 2024, the mass measured 3.1 × 2.4 × 1.8 cm, had a larger number of spicules and a ground-glass area on the periphery, and was without calcification. On 5/8/2021, it measured 1.5 x 0.9 cm.
In the left upper lobe, a subpleural area of ground-glass is preserved (series 5, image 47), measuring about 1.7 × 1.4 cm, with no change. 
An area of consolidation and ground glass in the left upper lobe adjacent to the interlobar pleura (series 5, images 66-83), with small bronchiectasis, measures 2.0 × 1.1 cm (previously 1.5 × 1.0 cm). The consolidation part has increased in comparison with chest CT on December 11, 2024. 
In the lower parts, there is thickening of the interlobular interstitium, reticular changes, and mosaic pneumatization. On the right, there are supradiaphragmatic centrilobular ground-glass nodules (tree-in-bud) (series 5, images 198-217), which are not previously identified. 
In the lung parenchyma, multiple nodules are identified, in series 5 presented as follows:
 In the right upper lobe:
- Calcified nodule measures 3 mm (series 5, image 30), with no change.
- Subpleural ground-glass nodule measures 3 mm (series 5, image 71), with no change.
In the right middle lobe:
- Perifissural solid nodule measures up to 8 mm (series 5, image 157), with no change.
In the right lower lobe:
 - A few subpleural solid nodules measure up to 3 mm (series 5, images 157-161), with no change.
In the left upper lobe:
- Subpleural solid nodule measures up to 3 mm (series 5, image 163), with no change.
In the left lower lobe:
- Subpleural solid nodule measures up to 6 mm (series 5, image 120), with no change.
Multiple calcified and solid micronodules up to 2 mm in both lungs with no change. 
No pleural effusion. Wall thickening of the subsegmental and segmental bronchi with secretion. The wall of the trachea and large bronchi is severely thickened.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass is identified. Subcarinal lymph node (station 7 lymph node) measures up to 1.2 cm in short axis, with no significant change. Other lymph nodes are multiple, up to 9 mm.

Heart:  No cardiomegaly.  No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels:  The thoracic aorta is not dilated; mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.1 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:  Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased bone tissue density in vertebrae at the T11-L2 level down to +113 HU, numerous rounded areas of low density in the vertebral bodies, previously identified. Osteosclerotic lesions on the right at the 7th rib, on the left at the 8th rib, with no change. There is flowing anterior osteophytosis, consistent with diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH).

Upper abdomen:  In the renal parenchyma, hypodense lesions with smooth margins and fluid density measure up to 5.9 × 5.7 cm on the right and 3.8 × 3.1 cm on the left (no change). These are consistent with cysts. No follow-up imaging recommended as incidental renal lesions without suspicious features are likely benign. Increase in size of a lymph node along the hepatoduodenal ligament to 1.2 cm in short axis, previously up to 8 mm. Increased spleen size, compared to December 11, 2024. Wall calcification of the abdominal aorta and its branches, including along the course of the superior mesenteric artery, which can predispose the patient to mesenteric ischemia. Portal vein dilation to 1.7 cm (no change), and splenic vein dilation to 1.0 cm (previously up to 9 mm).

IMPRESSION: 

1. No significant change with respect to the right apex mass compared to 12/11/2024, which remain significantly larger when compared with more remote studies. Similar enlarged subcarinal lymph node, no significant change.

2. Interval worsening in the form of an increase in the size of the consolidation area in the left upper lobe and the appearance of bronchiolitis in the supradiaphragmatic regions on the right. These changes may represent an inflammatory process. Multiple small nodules are without significant change. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history. Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.

3. Bronchial secretion and wall thickening may represent bronchitis. 

4. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Mildly dilated main pulmonary artery (3.1 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. Enlarged lymph node along the hepatoduodenal ligament (previously smaller), increased spleen size, dilation of the portal (stable) and splenic (with mild increase) veins. Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.



DISCLAIMER:  AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

In the left lower lobe:

In the right upper lobe:
- Micronodules measure 2-3 mm (image 35, 48, 72), no change.

No pleural effusion. The trachea and main bronchi are patent with a small amount of mucus.

3. Bronchial contents and bronchial wall thickening in both lungs, which may correspond to bronchitis.

4. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (2.9 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. Severe atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

7. Bilateral renal lesions; per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, follow-up is not required. Bilateral hydronephrosis. Bilateral nonobstructing renal calculi.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0

FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura:  Calcified micronodules in the left lung. Perifissural calcified nodule up to 7 mm in the right upper lobe. No nodules requiring follow-up.Thin linear atelectasis in segment 5 of the left upper lobe. On expiratory imaging, bilateral lobular air trapping is present. Gravity-dependent changes in the basal regions of the lungs. Thickening of the walls of segmental and subsegmental bronchi. No pleural effusion. 

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:  No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified. Single microcalcification in a paratracheal lymph node. Small hiatal hernia.

Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Findings of terminal bronchiolar obstruction with bilateral lobular air trapping, which may be consistent with bronchial asthma. Thickening of the walls of segmental and subsegmental bronchi.

3. There are no signs of interstitial lung disease.

4. Severe coronary artery calcification. Moderate aortic valve calcification and severe mitral valve calcification. Moderate atherosclerotic calcification of the thoracic aorta.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (34 mm) may be consistent with pulmonary hypertension.

6. Osteoblastic lesion in the L2 vertebral body, unchanged compared with the prior study.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.
